ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists specifically for lemongrass water. FOODON_03301710 (herbal tea food product) is the closest parent category covering plant-infused hot water beverages.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Herbal infusions) is the closest parent. No specific lemongrass water entry exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for lemongrass water as a food product. SNOMED CT coverage for herbal infusion beverages at this specificity is absent.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup of hot lemongrass water, approximately 240 ml, as typically served in a Singapore restaurant or café setting.

nutrient_source.title: Lemongrass tea / lemongrass infusion — brewed herbal tea, water-based; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy reference for brewed herbal tea and lemongrass composition
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct USDA FoodData Central entry exists for 'lemongrass water' as a brewed beverage. Values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for brewed herbal tea (water-based infusion) and published compositional data for lemongrass (Cymbopogon citratus) infusions. A hot water infusion of lemongrass stalks yields a beverage that is essentially water with trace volatile compounds and negligible macronutrients, consistent with published analyses of lemongrass tea. HPB Singapore Foodo does not carry a specific entry for lemongrass water. FAO/INFOODS SEA tables do not include a brewed lemongrass beverage entry. USDA SR Legacy brewed herbal tea (NDB 14355) is used as the primary compositional anchor.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of non-caloric and near-zero-calorie herbal beverages — analogous estimate based on brewed herbal tea reference
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for lemongrass water in the University of Sydney GI Database. Lemongrass water is a near-zero-calorie herbal infusion containing approximately 0.3 g carbohydrate per 100 ml with no glycemic carbohydrates. By analogy with brewed herbal teas and plain water, which have a GI of effectively zero, the GI is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel value per Veritas convention). Glycemic load per 240 ml serving = (1 × 0.72 g carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.007, rounded to 0.0. This is consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database classification of non-glycemic beverages.

hypertension.key_factor: Negligible sodium content (approximately 1 mg per 100 ml)
hypertension.guidance: Hot lemongrass water contains negligible sodium (~2.4 mg per 240 ml serving) and no added salt, making it an appropriate beverage choice for individuals managing hypertension; some preclinical evidence suggests lemongrass may have mild vasodilatory properties, though clinical evidence in humans remains limited.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Near-zero glycemic load and negligible carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Hot lemongrass water has a glycemic load of effectively zero per serving and contains no free sugars, making it a safe beverage for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; it does not raise postprandial blood glucose and can be consumed freely as part of a diabetes-friendly dietary pattern.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and negligible caloric content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Hot lemongrass water contains no saturated fat, no cholesterol, and negligible calories, posing no cardiovascular risk; lemongrass contains citral and other phytochemicals with antioxidant properties that may offer modest cardioprotective benefits, though large-scale human clinical trials are lacking.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hot lemongrass water (Nước Sả Nóng) is a simple herbal infusion made by steeping or briefly boiling fresh or dried lemongrass stalks (Cymbopogon citratus) in hot water. The resulting beverage is pale yellow to golden in colour with a clean, citrusy, and mildly floral aroma derived from the volatile compound citral present in lemongrass. The flavour is light, refreshing, and subtly lemony without any actual lemon or citrus fruit content. It is served hot and is popular across Southeast Asia, including Singapore, Vietnam, Thailand, and Malaysia, where it is consumed both as a daily wellness drink and as a digestive aid. In Singapore, it is commonly offered in Vietnamese and Southeast Asian restaurants as a house beverage or herbal drink option. Nutritionally, hot lemongrass water is essentially a near-zero-calorie beverage, providing approximately 1 kcal per 100 ml, with negligible protein, fat, and carbohydrate. It contains no free sugars and no glycemic carbohydrates, giving it an effective glycemic index of zero and a glycemic load of zero per serving. Sodium content is minimal at approximately 1 mg per 100 ml. The beverage is suitable for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease. It is naturally vegan, vegetarian, gluten-free, dairy-free, and halal. Lemongrass contains bioactive compounds including citral, geraniol, and limonene, which have demonstrated antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties in preclinical studies. The drink is prepared by simmering lemongrass stalks in water for 10–15 minutes or by steeping bruised stalks in boiling water, then straining and serving hot.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for brewed lemongrass water exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA tables. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy brewed herbal tea data (NDB 14355) and published compositional analyses of lemongrass infusions. The beverage is compositionally simple (essentially water with trace volatiles) and the estimation uncertainty is low, but the absence of a direct approved-source entry prevents a 'high' confidence rating.
notes: 1. energy_kcal is 1.0 kcal per 100 ml, reflecting trace volatile compounds and minimal dissolved solids from lemongrass infusion; this is not zero. 2. protein_g is naturally zero — lemongrass water is a water-based herbal infusion containing no protein-bearing macromolecules in the brewed liquid. 3. fat_g is naturally zero — no lipid compounds are present in detectable quantities in a water-based lemongrass infusion. 4. sugar_g is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy brewed herbal tea reference; no free sugars are present. 5. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero — the lemongrass solids are strained out before serving; no fibre passes into the liquid. 6. glycemic_index set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category low per Veritas convention for near-zero-GI beverages. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 as carbohydrate per serving is below 1 g. 7. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy brewed herbal tea reference for this beverage type. 8. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source reference. 9. vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source reference. 10. vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source reference. 11. vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source reference. 12. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the source reference. 13. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source reference. 14. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source reference. 15. phosphorus_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source reference. 16. magnesium_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source reference. 17. zinc_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the source reference. 18. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the source reference. 19. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a plant-based herbal infusion with no animal-derived ingredients. 20. The food is served at Co Chung Restaurant (Lorong Telok, Singapore) and listed on field survey data at SGD 5.68. The Vietnamese name 'Nước Sả nong' in the input has been preserved and normalised; 'nong' is a phonetic rendering of 'nóng' (hot). 21. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03301710 and FoodEx2 A00LC are closest_parent matches; no exact codes exist for lemongrass water specifically.
